---
title: "28 · From Table to Function: Why Function Approximation?"
subtitle: "Tables don't scale; replace v(s) by parameterised v(s; w)."
lesson-id: "mfrl.from-table-to-function"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.td-unified-view", "t5.partial-derivatives"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Comfortable with the master TD update from [Lesson 27](../27-td-unified-view/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute partial derivatives of a quadratic in many variables? — see
   [Tier 5, Lesson 5 — Partial Derivatives and the Gradient](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd).
3. Have you met dot products $\phi(s)^\top w = \sum_i \phi_i(s) w_i$? — see
   [Tier 4, Lesson 14 — Dot and Cross Product](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/14-dot-cross-product/index.qmd).
4. Can you imagine a state space too large to enumerate (chess, Go, robot
   joint angles, video pixels)?

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

So far you've stored value functions in **tables**: one number per state
(or per state-action pair). For a 5×5 grid that's $25$ entries — fine.

Drag the slider on grid size below. Memory cost: $|\mathcal{S}|$ floats for
$v(s)$.

```{ojs}
viewof gridSide = Inputs.range([5, 1000], {value: 100, step: 1, label: "Grid side N (states = N²):"})
viewof bytesPerCell = Inputs.select([4, 8], {value: 8, label: "bytes/value (4=float32, 8=float64):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const states = gridSide * gridSide;
  const memBytes = states * bytesPerCell;
  const memMB = memBytes / (1024 * 1024);
  const memGB = memMB / 1024;
  return html`<div style="font-family: system-ui; padding: 12px; border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px;">
    <p><strong>States:</strong> ${states.toLocaleString()}</p>
    <p><strong>Table memory:</strong> ${memBytes.toLocaleString()} bytes ≈ ${memMB.toFixed(2)} MB ≈ ${memGB.toFixed(4)} GB.</p>
    <p>Now imagine continuous state spaces (any real-valued sensor) — <em>infinite</em> states. The table doesn't fit.</p>
  </div>`;
}
```

A 1000×1000 grid is already 8 MB — manageable, but think bigger:

- A robot arm with 7 joint angles, each discretised into 100 bins: $10^{14}$
  states. At 8 bytes each, that's **800 terabytes**.
- Atari frames at 84×84 grayscale: $256^{7056}$ "states." Bigger than the
  number of atoms in the universe.
- Continuous joint torques: literally infinite states.

We need a different representation. Enter **function approximation**.

## Wonder

1. If we can't store one number per state, what *fewer* numbers might
   summarise an entire state space?
2. Many states are *similar* — neighbouring grid cells, similar sensor
   readings. Can a learning system *generalise* from one state to its
   neighbours, or must each state's value be learned in isolation?
3. By replacing the table with $\hat v(s; w)$ — a function with parameters
   $w$ — what do we gain? What do we lose?
4. The TD update from Lesson 27 wrote $v(s) \leftarrow v(s) + \alpha \delta$.
   How would you replace this with a "move $w$" update?

## Discover

Six problems. By the end you'll see why function approximation is *necessary*
for any RL problem you actually care about.

### Problem 1 — table memory

A continuous state space discretised to $N$ bins per dimension, with $d$
dimensions. How many table entries?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|\mathcal{S}| = N^d$. **Exponential in dimension** — the *curse of
dimensionality*. For $N = 100, d = 10$: $10^{20}$ entries. Forget it.
</details>

### Problem 2 — generalisation across states

In a tabular value function, what does updating $v(s_{17})$ tell you about
$v(s_{18})$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Nothing.** Each state has its own table entry; updates are isolated. To
learn $v(s_{18})$ you must visit $s_{18}$ separately and update it
separately. **Tabular learners cannot generalise.**
</details>

### Problem 3 — the linear function approximator

Define a *feature vector* $\phi(s) \in \mathbb{R}^d$ for each state — $d$
numbers that summarise $s$ (e.g., position, velocity, distance-to-goal).
Replace the table by
$$\hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)^\top w = \sum_{i=1}^d \phi_i(s) w_i.$$
Now the value function is determined by $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$. What is the
memory cost?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|w| = d$ floats. **Independent of $|\mathcal{S}|$.** A 5-dimensional
linear approximator stores 5 numbers — total — for an arbitrarily large
state space.
</details>

### Problem 4 — generalisation, again

When you update $w$ to lower the error on state $s$, does $\hat v$ change at
*other* states?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\hat v(s'; w) = \phi(s')^\top w$ — every state's value depends on the same
$w$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes — automatically.** Every state $s'$ that shares features with $s$
(non-zero $\phi(s) \cdot \phi(s')$) sees its $\hat v$ change too.
**Generalisation is built in.** This is the great gift of function
approximation: one update touches many states.
</details>

### Problem 5 — what you lose

State-by-state updates were *exact*: you could store any value function
exactly. With $d \ll |\mathcal{S}|$ parameters, you can no longer represent
every possible $v$. What do we call the resulting error?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Approximation error** (or *bias*). The function class
$\{\phi(s)^\top w : w \in \mathbb{R}^d\}$ is a $d$-dimensional subspace of
all possible $v: \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$. The true $v_\pi$ may not lie
in this subspace; the best $\hat v$ in the subspace is the *projection* of
$v_\pi$ onto the subspace, with error
$\|v_\pi - \Pi v_\pi\|$ in some norm.
**The trade is: less memory, more bias.** A larger $d$ (or a richer
function class) shrinks the bias.
</details>

### Problem 6 — the new update target

The tabular TD update was $v(s) \leftarrow v(s) + \alpha [r + \gamma v(s') - v(s)]$. The function-approximator analogue updates $w$, not $v$. Guess what
the update should be (we'll derive it formally in Lesson 29).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You want to nudge $\hat v(s; w)$ toward the TD target. Use chain-rule.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$w \leftarrow w + \alpha \, [r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w) - \hat v(s; w)] \, \nabla_w \hat v(s; w).$$
For linear FA, $\nabla_w \hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)$, so
$$w \leftarrow w + \alpha \, [r + \gamma \phi(s')^\top w - \phi(s)^\top w] \, \phi(s).$$
This is **gradient TD** (or *semi-gradient TD*, because the target also
depends on $w$ — see Lesson 29 for the catch). All updates now live in
parameter space.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
Function approximation buys you two things at once: **scalability** (memory
$\sim d$ instead of $|\mathcal{S}|$) and **generalisation** (one update
moves many states). The cost is **representational bias**: the true $v_\pi$
may not be expressible by your function class. Linear FA is the simplest
choice; deep neural networks are the richest.
:::

::: {.fun-fact}
**Tesauro's TD-Gammon** (1992) was the first deep success of function
approximation in RL. He represented $v$ with a **single-hidden-layer
neural network** and trained it with TD(λ). Within a year of training it
played at the level of the world's best human backgammon players. The 1995
follow-up paper showed it had taught the human community new opening
moves nobody had considered. **Function approximation made RL go from toy
grids to world-champion play in three years.**
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Value function approximation.** Replace the table $v: \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$
by a parameterised function
$$\hat v(s; w), \qquad w \in \mathbb{R}^d, \quad d \ll |\mathcal{S}|.$$

**Linear FA.** Define a feature vector $\phi(s) \in \mathbb{R}^d$ for each
state. Then
$$\hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)^\top w = \sum_{i=1}^d \phi_i(s) w_i.$$

**Action-value FA.** Likewise, $\hat q(s, a; w) = \phi(s, a)^\top w$ for
linear FA, or $\hat q(s, a; w) = \text{NN}(s, a; w)$ for neural FA.

**Trade.** Memory: $|w| = d$ instead of $|\mathcal{S}|$. Generalisation:
free across feature-similar states. Cost: representational bias when
$v_\pi$ falls outside the function class.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Many states share features in common —*
> *Update one, the others update too.*
> *A vector of weights replaces a table —*
> *Memory bounded, bias the price.*

## Apply

### Worked example — a 5×5 grid with 4 features

> A 5×5 grid. Goal at $(4, 4)$. We want to estimate $v_\pi(s)$ for some
> policy $\pi$. Tabular: 25 entries. Now define 4 features per state:
>
> - $\phi_1(s) = 1$ (constant — the "intercept" feature)
> - $\phi_2(s) = r/4$ (normalised row, so $\phi_2 \in [0, 1]$)
> - $\phi_3(s) = c/4$ (normalised col)
> - $\phi_4(s) = (|r - 4| + |c - 4|) / 8$ (normalised Manhattan distance to goal)
>
> So $w \in \mathbb{R}^4$. **Memory: 4 floats.**

A competent solver thinks like this:

1. **For state $s = (2, 1)$:** $\phi(s) = (1, 0.5, 0.25, (2 + 3)/8) = (1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.625)$.
2. **Suppose $w = (-0.1, 0.2, 0.2, -0.5)$:**
   $\hat v(s; w) = 1 \cdot (-0.1) + 0.5 \cdot 0.2 + 0.25 \cdot 0.2 + 0.625 \cdot (-0.5) = -0.1 + 0.1 + 0.05 - 0.3125 = -0.2625$.
3. **For the goal $s = (4, 4)$:** $\phi(s) = (1, 1, 1, 0)$,
   $\hat v(s; w) = -0.1 + 0.2 + 0.2 + 0 = 0.3$.
4. **The "distance-to-goal" feature has weight $-0.5$** — capturing the
   intuition that closer-to-goal states are higher value. After more
   training, that weight magnitude grows.

**Tabular vs FA on this grid:** 25 vs 4 numbers. For a 100×100 grid: 10,000
vs 4 numbers. **Same 4 weights** scale to any size. That's the punchline.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute $\hat v(s)$ for $s = (0, 0)$ in the worked-example setup
($w$ as above).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\phi(0,0) = (1, 0, 0, (4+4)/8) = (1, 0, 0, 1)$.
$\hat v = 1 \cdot (-0.1) + 0 + 0 + 1 \cdot (-0.5) = -0.6$. The corner
furthest from the goal has the most negative value.
</details>

**W2.** A tabular table for a 7-joint robot, 50 bins per joint, costs how
many floats?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$50^7 = 7.8 \times 10^{11}$ floats. At 4 bytes each: ~3 TB. *Per
state-value*. Action values multiply by $|A|$. Tabular is dead for
robotics.
</details>

**W3.** Why does the constant feature $\phi_1(s) = 1$ matter?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
It lets $\hat v$ have a non-zero baseline (intercept). Without it,
$\hat v(s)$ is forced through the origin in feature space — biased toward
zero. **Always include a constant feature** (or the equivalent in your
parameterisation).
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show that *tabular* learning is a special case of linear FA.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pick $\phi(s)$ to be a *one-hot* vector: $\phi_i(s) = 1$ if $s$ is the
$i$-th state, else 0.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
With $\phi(s) = e_s$ (one-hot indicator) and $w \in \mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{S}|}$, $\hat v(s; w) = w_s$ — exactly the table entry. So the
table is **linear FA with one-hot features**, and $d = |\mathcal{S}|$.
**Linear FA strictly generalises tabular**, with the parameter count chosen
to suit your memory budget.
</details>

**P2.** Compute the gradient $\nabla_w \hat v(s; w)$ for linear FA. Then
for the *quadratic* approximator $\hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)^\top W \phi(s)$
where $W$ is a $d \times d$ matrix.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Linear: $\nabla_w \hat v = \phi(s)$ (a $d$-vector).
Quadratic: $\nabla_W \hat v = \phi(s) \phi(s)^\top$ (a $d \times d$ matrix).
Computing the latter is $O(d^2)$ per state and gradient — not crippling for
modest $d$, but already much costlier than linear's $O(d)$.
</details>

**P3.** Compare *generalisation* in three FA choices for a continuous 1D
state $s \in [0, 1]$:
(a) Tabular with 100 bins.
(b) Linear FA with $\phi(s) = (1, s, s^2)$.
(c) Linear FA with 10 *radial basis features* $\phi_i(s) = \exp(-(s - c_i)^2 / 0.01)$ for centres $c_i = 0.05, 0.15, \ldots, 0.95$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For each, ask: how much does updating $\hat v(s_0)$ for one $s_0$ shift
$\hat v(s_1)$ for a far-away $s_1$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) **Tabular:** zero spillover unless $s_0$ and $s_1$ fall in the same
bin. No generalisation.
(b) **Polynomial linear FA:** every update moves *all* states' values
(every $\phi_i(s)$ is non-zero everywhere). Strong, possibly excessive,
generalisation — distant states are coupled.
(c) **Radial basis:** each feature is non-zero only near its centre. An
update at $s_0$ affects $\hat v$ near $s_0$ but barely at far-away $s_1$ —
**local generalisation**, the practical sweet spot.

The choice of features is the *engineering art* of classical RL. Deep nets
*learn* good features automatically — that's what makes them attractive.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A function class with $d$ parameters can represent at most a
$d$-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{S}|}$. The *projection
distance* from $v_\pi$ to this subspace is the *approximation error
floor* — no matter how well you optimise. State why convergence theorems
for FA-TD only guarantee approaching this floor, not zero.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
TD with linear FA on-policy converges to $\Pi v_\pi$ — the *projection* of
$v_\pi$ onto the feature subspace, where $\Pi$ projects under the
on-policy stationary distribution. So $\hat v$ approaches *the best linear
approximation of $v_\pi$ in the feature space*, not $v_\pi$ itself. The
non-zero floor $\|v_\pi - \Pi v_\pi\|$ is the *bias of the function class*
— irreducible without enriching the features.
</details>

**S2.** Why are *neural networks* attractive even though they have *more*
parameters than a small linear FA?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Two reasons. (i) Universal approximation: with enough hidden units, a
neural net can represent *any* continuous function — so the
approximation-error floor is 0 in principle. (ii) Learned features: the
hidden layers find good features automatically, so you don't have to
hand-craft $\phi$. The cost: optimisation is harder (non-convex loss),
convergence theorems are weaker, and the *deadly triad* (Lesson 30) bites.
The 2015 DQN paper (Mnih et al.) is the first really successful navigation
of that trade.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Function approximation always introduces error."** It introduces a
   *representation* error floor — bounded by the gap between $v_\pi$ and
   the function class. With enough parameters / a rich-enough class, that
   gap shrinks to (essentially) zero.
2. **"Updating $w$ is the same as updating one $v(s)$ entry."** No — it
   moves $\hat v$ at *every* state simultaneously. That is generalisation,
   and it's why FA-TD can outperform tabular even on small problems.
3. **"Linear FA can only represent linear functions of $s$."** It can
   represent any function expressible as $\phi(s)^\top w$ — including
   highly non-linear functions of $s$ if $\phi$ is non-linear (polynomial,
   radial basis, etc.). "Linear in $w$, possibly non-linear in $s$."
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else:

1. **Tables don't scale.** Memory is $|\mathcal{S}|$, exponential in
   dimension.
2. **Function approximation:** $\hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)^\top w$ for linear FA;
   neural net for non-linear. Memory $|w| = d$, often $d \ll |\mathcal{S}|$.
3. **Generalisation is the gift, bias is the price.** One update changes
   many states' values; the function class limits what $\hat v$ can be.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you state the linear FA equation $\hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)^\top w$
   and explain what each symbol does, without notes?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 8 practice problems without peeking past
   *Hint 1*?
3. Can you explain why tabular RL won't get you to chess, Go, or robot
   control?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Lesson 29 — TD with FA](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Working P3 — feature design is the practical art that
  distinguishes good RL engineers.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problems 3 and 4. The "$d$ parameters,
  generalisation across feature-similar states" idea is the load-bearing
  insight.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[MFRL — 27, TD Methods Unified](../27-td-unified-view/index.qmd)** —
  the master TD update we'll soon promote to parameter space.
- **[Tier 5, Lesson 5 — Partial Derivatives](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)** — for the gradient $\nabla_w \hat v$
  in Problem 6.
- **[Tier 4, Lesson 14 — Dot and Cross Product](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/14-dot-cross-product/index.qmd)** — for $\phi(s)^\top w$.

### What this unlocks

- **[MFRL — 29, TD with FA](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd)** — the formal derivation of the gradient-TD update.
- **[MFRL — 30, Q-Learning with FA](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)** —
  the deadly triad and Baird's counterexample.
- **[MFRL — 31, DQN](../31-deep-q-network/index.qmd)** — neural-net function
  approximation in practice.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every modern RL agent uses function approximation. Deep
networks are the dominant choice (DQN, A3C, PPO, SAC, IMPALA, MuZero), but
linear FA still appears in finance, control engineering, and constrained
embedded systems where interpretability or guarantee of convergence
matters.
See [gradient descent](/glossary.qmd#gradient-descent).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Systems.** Storing $|\mathcal{S}|$ values in a table — and *visiting*
them all — is what kills tabular RL on real systems. Once $|\mathcal{S}|$
exceeds the system's RAM, the choice of function approximator becomes
*the* central design question for the engineer.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this track
expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Source:** Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement
  Learning* (Springer 2025), Chapter 8 §8.1. Cited freely; never transcribed.
  This lesson is the *transition* lesson — Chapter 8's deeper material is
  expanded in lessons 29-31, authored by the next agent.
- **Touchstones honoured:** Russian school problem-first sequencing,
  Better Explained "memory vs generalisation" framing, Bhāskara verse
  for the trade.
- **Self-study guarantees:** pre-flight ✓, multiple representations
  (widget, formula, English, table-vs-FA comparison) ✓, graduated hints ✓,
  worked solutions ✓, common-confusions ✓, self-assessment ✓.
:::
